title: A Mother Is a Magistrate: Why Duress Is No Defense for Abortion
author: Sam Jones
orientation: Sam Jones answers Bradley Pierce's defense of narrowly tailored duress clauses in equal protection legislation, arguing that a mother is a magistrate over her preborn child and therefore cannot lawfully be excused for choosing abortion under threat. The piece is aimed at abolitionists, legislators, and pro-life thinkers wrestling with how mercy and statutory duty should be drafted in equal-protection bills.
audiences: Abolitionist legislators drafting equal protection bills
Christians wrestling with the theology of magistracy and authority
Lawyers and policy analysts working on homicide statutes
Pastors and laypeople thinking about mercy versus justice

questions.question: What is the central disagreement Sam Jones has with Bradley Pierce over duress clauses?
questions.audience: Abolitionist legislators drafting equal protection bills
questions.answer: Jones grants that Bradley Pierce's intent is sincere and his case-law work valuable, but says Pierce makes a foundational error about magistracy rather than merely a legal terminology dispute. Jones frames the issue as theological: duress cannot excuse a magistrate from her office, and a mother is a magistrate over the child in her womb. Pierce's narrowly tailored duress clauses, in Jones's view, encode partiality into equal protection law itself.
questions.quote: This is not merely a legal disagreement over terminology. It is a theological disagreement over authority, responsibility, and justice. The fundamental problem is this: duress cannot excuse abdication of office, and a mother is not merely a private citizen—she is a magistrate over her child.

questions.question: Why does the article call a mother a 'magistrate' over her preborn child?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with the theology of magistracy and authority
questions.answer: Jones applies the doctrine of the lesser magistrate, which holds that closer jurisdictions carry greater responsibility to interpose against injustice. Because a mother literally carries her child, she is the most immediate and personal authority over that life, comparable to a sheriff over a county or a father over a household. That delegated, God-given office is what makes abortion under duress a betrayal rather than a forgivable lapse.
questions.quote: And a mother—who carries within her the life of her child—is the immediate and personal magistrate of that child. If a governor is accountable for protecting citizens within his state, how much more is a mother responsible for the life within her own womb?

questions.question: How does Jones respond to the argument that abortion under duress is just triage because both mother and child would otherwise die?
questions.answer: Jones rejects the triage framing: a magistrate's duty is not to calculate survival odds but to protect the one entrusted to her, even at cost of her own life. Drawing on Blackstone, he argues a person ought to die rather than escape by murdering an innocent. If both mother and child die in the act of refusing to abort, the magistrate has fulfilled her office; if she kills the child to save herself, she has abandoned it.
questions.quote: The lesser magistrate’s duty is not to calculate who will live—it is to protect the one under their care at all costs. If a magistrate dies in the act of interposition, he has fulfilled his office. If he takes the life of the one he was called to defend, even to save himself, he has abandoned it.

questions.question: What is the difference between physical coercion and volitional coercion in this article?
questions.audience: Lawyers and policy analysts working on homicide statutes
questions.answer: Physical coercion is when a woman's body is acted upon against her will—she is held down, drugged, or has abortion pills administered without consent—so her will is overridden and she is a victim, not a perpetrator. Volitional coercion (commonly called duress) is when she is threatened with severe consequences but still makes a choice, such as taking the pill to avoid being killed. Physical coercion removes agency; volitional coercion only pressures it, and that distinction is morally and legally decisive.
questions.quote: The key difference: physical coercion removes agency. Volitional coercion pressures it.

questions.question: Does the article say a woman who is physically forced to undergo an abortion has committed homicide?
questions.answer: No. Jones is explicit that when a woman is physically overridden—held down during a surgical abortion, forcibly drugged, or given abortion pills without consent—she is a victim, not a perpetrator, and what happened is not homicide on her part. The article reserves culpability for cases where the woman still made the decision, even under threat.
questions.quote: In such cases, the woman’s will is overridden. Her body is used, but she did not govern the act. She is a victim, not a perpetrator. This is not homicide on her part.

questions.question: Why does the author oppose writing a duress exception into equal protection statutes specifically?
questions.audience: Abolitionist legislators drafting equal protection bills
questions.answer: Jones argues that equal protection means homicide laws apply to everyone equally, including mothers, so if a father or doctor cannot kill a child under threat, neither can a mother. Encoding a duress carve-out for the one person with jurisdiction over the victim builds partiality into the statute itself and tells mothers that fear can suspend their duty. That, he says, betrays the child the law is meant to protect.
questions.quote: Equal protection means that the laws against homicide apply to everyone equally—including to mothers. If a father or doctor may not kill a child under threat, neither may the mother.

questions.question: Where does mercy fit in if the law refuses a duress defense?
questions.audience: Pastors and laypeople thinking about mercy versus justice
questions.answer: Jones insists that rejecting a statutory duress clause does not eliminate mercy—it relocates it. Prosecutors can decline to press charges, juries can recommend leniency, and governors can issue pardons, all without writing partiality into the homicide code. The principle is that the statute should speak with no partiality while case-by-case discretion handles individual circumstances.
questions.quote: This does not mean there is no place for mercy. A prosecutor may decline to press charges. A jury may recommend leniency. A governor may issue a pardon. But none of these merciful responses require that we encode partiality into the law itself.

questions.question: How does the article connect American constitutional theory to a mother's authority over her child?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with the theology of magistracy and authority
questions.answer: Jones argues that a constitutional republic distributes authority among the people rather than concentrating it in a king, so each citizen bears delegated authority under God and law. That is why a president cannot enter a person's home without warrant—the householder is the magistrate there. By the same logic, the womb is under the mother's immediate jurisdiction, making her, not the abuser threatening her, the responsible magistrate over the child's life.
questions.quote: In America, we live in a constitutional republic. This means that authority is not concentrated in a king, but distributed among the people themselves. Each citizen bears delegated authority under God and law. This is why a president cannot simply enter your home without invitation or a warrant—because in your household, you are the magistrate.

questions.question: What does Jones say a duress clause communicates to mothers as a matter of message?
questions.audience: Pastors and laypeople thinking about mercy versus justice
questions.answer: Jones reads a statutory duress carve-out as effectively saying, 'If you are afraid enough, you may abdicate your duty to protect.' He calls that a betrayal of the child rather than justice, because it tells mothers their fear matters more than their duty and more than their child's life. The statute, in his view, should instead demand courage and refuse to suspend the magistrate's duty out of fear.
questions.quote: To enshrine duress as a legal defense in abortion law is to tell mothers that their fear matters more than their duty—and more than their child’s life.

questions.question: Does Jones think existing homicide law already supports his position?
questions.audience: Lawyers and policy analysts working on homicide statutes
questions.answer: Yes. He points to Blackstone's principle that a person ought rather to die himself than escape by the murder of an innocent, and notes this rule is already embedded in homicide statutes that refuse duress as a defense for murder. His argument is that abolitionist equal-protection bills should preserve, not undermine, that long-standing common-law structure when the victim happens to be preborn.
questions.quote: William Blackstone famously wrote that a man “ought rather to die himself than escape by the murder of an innocent.” This principle has shaped just law for centuries and is rightly applied in homicide statutes that do not permit duress as a defense for murder.

questions.question: What does the article say about whether sympathetic circumstances make volitional coercion morally neutral?
questions.answer: Jones is direct that volitional coercion is not morally neutral even when circumstances evoke deep sympathy. The woman still chooses abortion under pressure, and the child—over whom she has legal and moral jurisdiction—is still dead. Sympathy may shape mercy in individual cases, but it cannot reframe the act as something other than the abdication of a magistrate's duty.
questions.quote: Volitional coercion is not morally neutral. The woman chooses abortion, even under pressure, and thereby acts against her duty as a magistrate. Her circumstances may evoke sympathy. But the child is still dead—and the one with legal and moral jurisdiction over that child made the decision.

questions.question: What concrete legislative posture does the article ultimately recommend?
questions.audience: Abolitionist legislators drafting equal protection bills
questions.answer: Jones recommends that equal protection statutes treat physical coercion as exonerating but refuse to write a volitional-duress defense into the text. Juries should be allowed to weigh details, and mercy can be extended case by case through prosecutorial, jury, or executive discretion. The statute itself should hold firm with no partiality, treating a child's life as no less valuable because his mother is afraid.
questions.quote:
But if we truly believe in equal protection, the law must hold firm:

• Physical coercion is not her crime.

• Volitional choice, even under threat, is.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Foundational explainer for the equal protection framework this article applies to duress clauses.

related.title: No Exceptions
related.reason: Same-site argument against statutory carve-outs in abolitionist legislation, directly parallel to opposing duress clauses.

related.title: Abolitionists Must Stand Firm to Oppose Murder, Love Murderers
related.reason: Companion piece on holding mothers morally accountable while extending love, mirroring this article's mercy-versus-statute distinction.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Frames the abolitionist identity that Jones is defending against pro-life compromise positions.

related.title: How Shall an Abolitionist Vote?
related.reason: Same author (Sam Jones) reasoning about exceptions and political compromise from the abolitionist vantage.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Develops the mercy-and-justice tension in abolitionist thought that this article applies to duress provisions.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Cross-site rebuttal of compromise positions in equal protection drafting, parallel to the Bradley Pierce rebuttal here.
